texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.4 Search interface and search algorithms
texts.text: B.4 Search interface and search algorithms

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Search algorithms are implemented over the Checkpoint interface. Parameters to a search algorithm can be specified both in the arguments to search() when invoking a compiled search space object as well as in branchpoint parameters specified as arguments to branchpoint() and branchpoint_choose() within the ENCOMPASS function.
texts.text: Search algorithms are implemented over the Checkpoint interface. Parameters to a search algorithm can be specified both in the arguments to search() when invoking a compiled search space object as well as in branchpoint parameters specified as arguments to branchpoint() and branchpoint_choose() within the ENCOMPASS function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ENCOMPASS provides several common search algorithms out-of-the-box. The async implementations take advantage of the I/O-bound nature of LLM applications, whereas the non-async implementations use multithreaded parallelism, which the user can disable if they wish (e.g., to prevent race conditions when there are NoCopy variables). Here is the complete list of search algorithms in the current version of ENCOMPASS:
texts.text: ENCOMPASS provides several common search algorithms out-of-the-box. The async implementations take advantage of the I/O-bound nature of LLM applications, whereas the non-async implementations use multithreaded parallelism, which the user can disable if they wish (e.g., to prevent race conditions when there are NoCopy variables). Here is the complete list of search algorithms in the current version of ENCOMPASS:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Depth-first search (DFS)
texts.text: Depth-first search (DFS)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Breadth-first search (BFS)
texts.text: Breadth-first search (BFS)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Best-first search (BeFS)
texts.text: Best-first search (BeFS)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Beam search
texts.text: Beam search

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Monte-Carlo tree search (MCTS), with a given value function
texts.text: Monte-Carlo tree search (MCTS), with a given value function

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Reexpand best-first search, a variant of BeFS where an expanded node can be expanded again. This was used in Case Study 3 (Appendix A.2).
texts.text: Reexpand best-first search, a variant of BeFS where an expanded node can be expanded again. This was used in Case Study 3 (Appendix A.2).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Explorative reexpand best-first search, a variant of reexpand BeFS where a UCB-like exploration bonus is added to the score.
texts.text: Explorative reexpand best-first search, a variant of reexpand BeFS where a UCB-like exploration bonus is added to the score.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The user can also implement and register their custom search algorithm by subclassing the abstract Search class. Here, we provide a template for defining and registering a custom search algorithm:
texts.text: The user can also implement and register their custom search algorithm by subclassing the abstract Search class. Here, we provide a template for defining and registering a custom search algorithm:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 @register_search_algo(is_async=False) # or `is_async=True` if subclassing `AsyncSearch` 2 class MySearch(Search): # or `MySearch(AsyncSearch)` 3 name = "my_search" 4 param_names = ["param1", "param2"] # names of branchpoint parameters that I will use 5 6 def __init__(self, *, config1, config2, default_param1, default_param2): 7 self.config1 = config1 8 self.config2 = config2 9 self.default_param1 = default_param1 10 self.default_param2 = default_param2 11 12 def search_generator( 13 self, 14 init_program_state: Checkpoint 15 ) -> Generator[tuple[Any, ScoreWithCallback], None, None]: 16 # or `async def async_search_generator(self, init_program_state: AsyncCheckpoint)` 17 # if subclassing `AsyncSearch` 18 """ 19 Yields pairs (return_value: Any, score_with_callback: ScoreWithCallback) 20 as they are found. 21 22 ScoreWithCallback is a wrapper around a program state's score 23 - it is needed for group evaluation to work properly. 24 """ 25 # REPLACE CODE BELOW WITH YOUR CUSTOM SEARCH ALGORITHM 26 next_program_states = init_program_state.parallel_step_sampler(...) 27 for next_program_state in next_program_states: 28 param1 = next_program_state.get_branchpoint_param("param1", self.default_param1) 29 if next_program_state.has_return_value: 30 yield next_program_state.return_value, next_program_state._score_with_callback 31 ... 32 ...
texts.text: 1 @register_search_algo(is_async=False) # or `is_async=True` if subclassing `AsyncSearch` 2 class MySearch(Search): # or `MySearch(AsyncSearch)` 3 name = "my_search" 4 param_names = ["param1", "param2"] # names of branchpoint parameters that I will use 5 6 def __init__(self, *, config1, config2, default_param1, default_param2): 7 self.config1 = config1 8 self.config2 = config2 9 self.default_param1 = default_param1 10 self.default_param2 = default_param2 11 12 def search_generator( 13 self, 14 init_program_state: Checkpoint 15 ) -> Generator[tuple[Any, ScoreWithCallback], None, None]: 16 # or `async def async_search_generator(self, init_program_state: AsyncCheckpoint)` 17 # if subclassing `AsyncSearch` 18 """ 19 Yields pairs (return_value: Any, score_with_callback: ScoreWithCallback) 20 as they are found. 21 22 ScoreWithCallback is a wrapper around a program state's score 23 - it is needed for group evaluation to work properly. 24 """ 25 # REPLACE CODE BELOW WITH YOUR CUSTOM SEARCH ALGORITHM 26 next_program_states = init_program_state.parallel_step_sampler(...) 27 for next_program_state in next_program_states: 28 param1 = next_program_state.get_branchpoint_param("param1", self.default_param1) 29 if next_program_state.has_return_value: 30 yield next_program_state.return_value, next_program_state._score_with_callback 31 ... 32 ...

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C The ENCOMPASS compiler
texts.text: C The ENCOMPASS compiler

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ENCOMPASS compiler syntactically transforms an ENCOMPASS function into an equivalent regular Python program by conversion to continuation-passing style (CPS) and applying tail-call optimization.
texts.text: The ENCOMPASS compiler syntactically transforms an ENCOMPASS function into an equivalent regular Python program by conversion to continuation-passing style (CPS) and applying tail-call optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For simplicity, we only describe how we compile ENCOMPASS functions that are not async. The compiler transformations for async ENCOMPASS functions are nearly identical.
texts.text: For simplicity, we only describe how we compile ENCOMPASS functions that are not async. The compiler transformations for async ENCOMPASS functions are nearly identical.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C.1 CPS for branchpoints
texts.text: C.1 CPS for branchpoints

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this subsection, we describe how to convert a piece of code containing branchpoints (but not any of the other EnCompass keyword primitives) into CPS.
texts.text: In this subsection, we describe how to convert a piece of code containing branchpoints (but not any of the other EnCompass keyword primitives) into CPS.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In its simplest form, transforming a piece of code into CPS results in a function
texts.text: In its simplest form, transforming a piece of code into CPS results in a function

texts.label: code
texts.orig: cps_function(frame: Frame, rest: Frame -> None) -> None
texts.text: cps_function(frame: Frame, rest: Frame -> None) -> None

texts.label: text
texts.orig: which runs the piece of code on the variable mapping frame to get a new variable mapping, followed by calling the callback rest on that new variable mapping. Here, the callback rest , called the continuation , represents the rest of the program.
texts.text: which runs the piece of code on the variable mapping frame to get a new variable mapping, followed by calling the callback rest on that new variable mapping. Here, the callback rest , called the continuation , represents the rest of the program.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a piece of code that doesn't contain any branchpoints, it suffices to transform variable accesses and assignments to explicitly use frame . For example,
texts.text: For a piece of code that doesn't contain any branchpoints, it suffices to transform variable accesses and assignments to explicitly use frame . For example,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is compiled into
texts.text: is compiled into

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 frame['x'] = 1 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 rest(frame)
texts.text: 1 frame['x'] = 1 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 rest(frame)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that we omit the def cps_function(frame, rest): in the compiled code, so technically we're compiling to the body of the CPS function. We will call this the CPS body to distinguish it from the CPS function . We defer the job of wrapping the CPS body into a function to whoever asked for the compilation. This simplifies the issue of naming CPS functions and referring to them with the correct name.
texts.text: Note that we omit the def cps_function(frame, rest): in the compiled code, so technically we're compiling to the body of the CPS function. We will call this the CPS body to distinguish it from the CPS function . We defer the job of wrapping the CPS body into a function to whoever asked for the compilation. This simplifies the issue of naming CPS functions and referring to them with the correct name.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the compiled CPS function explicitly runs the continuation rest(frame) , adding a branchpoint immediately after the piece of code amounts to modifying the continuation to incorporate the search process. So we replace rest(frame) with branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) , which defines the rest of the program when we hit a branchpoint, where rest here now represents the rest of the program when we resume from the branchpoint. Taking the example above and adding a branchpoint at the end,
texts.text: Since the compiled CPS function explicitly runs the continuation rest(frame) , adding a branchpoint immediately after the piece of code amounts to modifying the continuation to incorporate the search process. So we replace rest(frame) with branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) , which defines the rest of the program when we hit a branchpoint, where rest here now represents the rest of the program when we resume from the branchpoint. Taking the example above and adding a branchpoint at the end,

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 x = 1 2 y = x + 1 3 branchpoint()
texts.text: 1 x = 1 2 y = x + 1 3 branchpoint()

texts.label: text
texts.orig: gets compiled into the following CPS body:
texts.text: gets compiled into the following CPS body:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 frame['x'] = 1 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 frame['x'] = 1 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) first stores the current program state (frame, rest) as a node in the search tree, then uses the search algorithm to decide a node (frame1, rest1 ) in the search tree to expand, and call rest1(frame1.clone()) to run the rest of the program resuming from the branchpoint that saved the state (frame1, rest1) . Cloning frame1 is needed because otherwise multiple calls to rest1(frame1) would modify the same frame1 object.
texts.text: Here, branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) first stores the current program state (frame, rest) as a node in the search tree, then uses the search algorithm to decide a node (frame1, rest1 ) in the search tree to expand, and call rest1(frame1.clone()) to run the rest of the program resuming from the branchpoint that saved the state (frame1, rest1) . Cloning frame1 is needed because otherwise multiple calls to rest1(frame1) would modify the same frame1 object.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far we've only defined how to transform programs with no branchpoints and programs with one branchpoint at the end. The transformation of a general program with branchpoints in arbitrary
texts.text: So far we've only defined how to transform programs with no branchpoints and programs with one branchpoint at the end. The transformation of a general program with branchpoints in arbitrary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: locations can be defined recursively with these two base cases. For example, a program with a branchpoint in the middle,
texts.text: locations can be defined recursively with these two base cases. For example, a program with a branchpoint in the middle,

texts.text: branchpoint() and

texts.text: where we can apply the recursive transformation rule for concatenation,

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 def rest(frame): 2 CPS ( B ) 3 CPS ( A )
texts.text: 1 def rest(frame): 2 CPS ( B ) 3 CPS ( A )

texts.text: to obtain the CPS body

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 def rest(frame): 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 finish_callback(frame) 4 frame['x'] = 1 5 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 def rest(frame): 2 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 3 finish_callback(frame) 4 frame['x'] = 1 5 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that we have replaced rest(frame) with finish_callback(frame) in the compilation of B to avoid name collision with the def rest(frame) . As a result, the compiled CPS function of the complete top-level program (AST root node) also has to reflect this name change in its signature: top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) instead of top_level_cps_function(frame, rest) . So, if Listing 17 is our entire program, then its CPS function is
texts.text: Note that we have replaced rest(frame) with finish_callback(frame) in the compilation of B to avoid name collision with the def rest(frame) . As a result, the compiled CPS function of the complete top-level program (AST root node) also has to reflect this name change in its signature: top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) instead of top_level_cps_function(frame, rest) . So, if Listing 17 is our entire program, then its CPS function is

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 def top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback): 2 def rest(frame): 3 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 4 finish_callback(frame) 5 frame['x'] = 1 6 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 def top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback): 2 def rest(frame): 3 frame['y'] = frame['x'] + 1 4 finish_callback(frame) 5 frame['x'] = 1 6 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a more complicated example, consider the following code:
texts.text: As a more complicated example, consider the following code:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 i = 0 # A 2 branchpoint() # A 3 j = 0 # B 4 while i < 10: # B 5 j -= 1 # B -X 6 branchpoint() # B -X 7 i += 1 # B -Y 8 print(j) # C
texts.text: 1 i = 0 # A 2 branchpoint() # A 3 j = 0 # B 4 while i < 10: # B 5 j -= 1 # B -X 6 branchpoint() # B -X 7 i += 1 # B -Y 8 print(j) # C

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We've chunked up the statements at the top level into 3 pieces: A , B and C . Each chunk consists of zero or more branchpoint-free statements followed by a statement containing a branchpoint, except for the last chunk C which is branchpoint-free. We apply the concatenation rule to A and ( B ; C ) , which recursively applies the concatenation rule to B and C . This then recursively compiles the last statement of B -the while loop. Compiling the while loop using the while loop rule recursively compiles the body of the while loop using the concatenation rule on the chunks X and Y .
texts.text: We've chunked up the statements at the top level into 3 pieces: A , B and C . Each chunk consists of zero or more branchpoint-free statements followed by a statement containing a branchpoint, except for the last chunk C which is branchpoint-free. We apply the concatenation rule to A and ( B ; C ) , which recursively applies the concatenation rule to B and C . This then recursively compiles the last statement of B -the while loop. Compiling the while loop using the while loop rule recursively compiles the body of the while loop using the concatenation rule on the chunks X and Y .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The concatenation rule as applied to chunks X and Y gives
texts.text: The concatenation rule as applied to chunks X and Y gives

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 def rest(frame): 2 # CPS body of Y 3 frame['i'] += 1 4 continue_callback(frame) 5 # CPS body of X 6 frame['j'] -= 1 7 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 def rest(frame): 2 # CPS body of Y 3 frame['i'] += 1 4 continue_callback(frame) 5 # CPS body of X 6 frame['j'] -= 1 7 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where to avoid name collision we replaced rest(frame) with continue_callback(frame) .
texts.text: where to avoid name collision we replaced rest(frame) with continue_callback(frame) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Applying the while loop rule gives
texts.text: Applying the while loop rule gives

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 def body_cps_function(frame, continue_callback, break_callback): 2 # CPS body of (X; Y) (from above) 3 def rest(frame): 4 # CPS body of Y 5 frame['i'] += 1 6 continue_callback(frame) 7 # CPS body of X 8 frame['j'] -= 1 9 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) 10 def while_cps_function(frame, rest): 11 if frame['i'] < 10: 12 body_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: while_cps_function(frame, rest)) 13 else: 14 rest(frame) 15 while_cps_function(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 def body_cps_function(frame, continue_callback, break_callback): 2 # CPS body of (X; Y) (from above) 3 def rest(frame): 4 # CPS body of Y 5 frame['i'] += 1 6 continue_callback(frame) 7 # CPS body of X 8 frame['j'] -= 1 9 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) 10 def while_cps_function(frame, rest): 11 if frame['i'] < 10: 12 body_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: while_cps_function(frame, rest)) 13 else: 14 rest(frame) 15 while_cps_function(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, applying the concatenation rule twice in ( A ; ( B ; C )) gives the CPS body of the entire program:
texts.text: Finally, applying the concatenation rule twice in ( A ; ( B ; C )) gives the CPS body of the entire program:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 # CPS body of (A; B; C) 2 def rest(frame): 3 # CPS body of (B; C) 4 def rest(frame): 5 # CPS body of C 6 print(frame['j']) 7 finish_callback(frame) 8 # CPS body of B 9 frame['j'] = 0 10 ... # CPS body of the while loop (from above) 11 # CPS body of A 12 frame['i'] = 0 13 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)
texts.text: 1 # CPS body of (A; B; C) 2 def rest(frame): 3 # CPS body of (B; C) 4 def rest(frame): 5 # CPS body of C 6 print(frame['j']) 7 finish_callback(frame) 8 # CPS body of B 9 frame['j'] = 0 10 ... # CPS body of the while loop (from above) 11 # CPS body of A 12 frame['i'] = 0 13 branchpoint_callback(frame, rest)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And, as usual, to get the CPS function of this program, we simply wrap the above CPS body into a def top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) function.
texts.text: And, as usual, to get the CPS function of this program, we simply wrap the above CPS body into a def top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the general solution to dealing with name collision is to add the correct version of rest( frame) to the end of each 'body' in the AST during preprocessing, so that we don't have to deal with it during conversion to CPS:
texts.text: Note that the general solution to dealing with name collision is to add the correct version of rest( frame) to the end of each 'body' in the AST during preprocessing, so that we don't have to deal with it during conversion to CPS:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At the end of the top-level program, add finish_callback(frame) during preprocessing. During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of a top-level program will be top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) instead of top_level_cps_function(frame, rest) .
texts.text: At the end of the top-level program, add finish_callback(frame) during preprocessing. During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of a top-level program will be top_level_cps_function(frame, finish_callback) instead of top_level_cps_function(frame, rest) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At the end of the body of a for/while loop, add continue_callback(frame) during preprocessing. During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body of a for/while loop will be body_cps_function(frame, continue_callback, break_callback) instead of body_cps_function(frame, rest) . Note that this also specifies the names of the callbacks that continue and break statements in the body get converted to during conversion to CPS - two birds with one stone.
texts.text: At the end of the body of a for/while loop, add continue_callback(frame) during preprocessing. During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body of a for/while loop will be body_cps_function(frame, continue_callback, break_callback) instead of body_cps_function(frame, rest) . Note that this also specifies the names of the callbacks that continue and break statements in the body get converted to during conversion to CPS - two birds with one stone.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At the end of the body of an if or an else , add if_else_callback(frame ) . During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body
texts.text: At the end of the body of an if or an else , add if_else_callback(frame ) . During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: of an if will be if_body_cps_function(frame, if_else_callback) instead of if_body_cps_function(frame, rest) , and similarly for else .
texts.text: of an if will be if_body_cps_function(frame, if_else_callback) instead of if_body_cps_function(frame, rest) , and similarly for else .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At the end of the body of a function, add return_callback(frame.caller_frame) . During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body of a function will be function_body_cps_function(frame, return_callback) instead of function_body_cps_function(frame, rest) .
texts.text: At the end of the body of a function, add return_callback(frame.caller_frame) . During conversion to CPS, the signature of the CPS function of the body of a function will be function_body_cps_function(frame, return_callback) instead of function_body_cps_function(frame, rest) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Weare now ready to formally write down the full set of transformations for EnCompass programs with the simplest version of EnCompass that only has branchpoints. For simplicity, we only describe the transformations done for synchronous code (no async/await) where only loops, if/else statements and function definitions have branchpoints ( with , try-except, and match statements are all branchpointfree).
texts.text: Weare now ready to formally write down the full set of transformations for EnCompass programs with the simplest version of EnCompass that only has branchpoints. For simplicity, we only describe the transformations done for synchronous code (no async/await) where only loops, if/else statements and function definitions have branchpoints ( with , try-except, and match statements are all branchpointfree).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Preprocessing The preprocessing stage consists of the following steps:
texts.text: Preprocessing The preprocessing stage consists of the following steps:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Convert all names var to frame[' var '] .
texts.text: Convert all names var to frame[' var '] .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Add finish_callback(frame) to the end of the program.
texts.text: Add finish_callback(frame) to the end of the program.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Add continue_callback(frame) to the end of the body of every for/while loop.
texts.text: Add continue_callback(frame) to the end of the body of every for/while loop.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Add if_else_callback(frame) to the end of the body of every branch of every if-else statement.
texts.text: Add if_else_callback(frame) to the end of the body of every branch of every if-else statement.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Add return_callback(frame.caller_frame) to the end of the body of every function that doesn't already end in a return statement.
texts.text: Add return_callback(frame.caller_frame) to the end of the body of every function that doesn't already end in a return statement.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. Make the following replacements:
texts.text: Make the following replacements:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · continue → continue_callback(frame) · break → break_callback(frame) · return rv → return_callback(frame.caller_frame, rv)
texts.text: continue → continue_callback(frame) · break → break_callback(frame) · return rv → return_callback(frame.caller_frame, rv)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conversion to CPS Here are the general transformation rules for compiling a top-level program or the body of a function, after the preprocessing steps described above have been completed.
texts.text: Conversion to CPS Here are the general transformation rules for compiling a top-level program or the body of a function, after the preprocessing steps described above have been completed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Base case - branchpoint-free: If A has no branchpoints, make no changes. In other words, CPS ( A ) = A .
texts.text: Base case - branchpoint-free: If A has no branchpoints, make no changes. In other words, CPS ( A ) = A .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Base case - branchpoint: A branchpoint
texts.text: Base case - branchpoint: A branchpoint

texts.text: 1 branchpoint()

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C.2 Tail-call optimization
texts.text: C.2 Tail-call optimization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are two issues with the compiled CPS representation. One issue is performance - the extra function calls cause overhead, and long for/while loops become deep recursive calls that can exceed Python's recursion depth limit. The second issue is that defining the search algorithm by defining branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) is unnatural and difficult. Typically, a search algorithm is implemented assuming access to a step method that returns a child of a node, new_state = step(state) .
texts.text: There are two issues with the compiled CPS representation. One issue is performance - the extra function calls cause overhead, and long for/while loops become deep recursive calls that can exceed Python's recursion depth limit. The second issue is that defining the search algorithm by defining branchpoint_callback(frame, rest) is unnatural and difficult. Typically, a search algorithm is implemented assuming access to a step method that returns a child of a node, new_state = step(state) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We solve both issues via tail-call optimization . More specifically, every branchpoint_callback( frame, rest) is replaced with return frame, rest , and rest(frame) no longer resumes from a branchpoint to execute the rest of the program, but only executes until the next branchpoint is hit, at which point the frame, rest at that branchpoint is returned. In other words, new_frame, new_rest = rest(frame.clone()) is exactly the new_state = step(state) that we need, where we identify state with (frame, rest) .
texts.text: We solve both issues via tail-call optimization . More specifically, every branchpoint_callback( frame, rest) is replaced with return frame, rest , and rest(frame) no longer resumes from a branchpoint to execute the rest of the program, but only executes until the next branchpoint is hit, at which point the frame, rest at that branchpoint is returned. In other words, new_frame, new_rest = rest(frame.clone()) is exactly the new_state = step(state) that we need, where we identify state with (frame, rest) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With this modification, reproducing the execution of the program when all branchpoints are ignored now involves a while loop that keeps stepping until the program finishes:
texts.text: With this modification, reproducing the execution of the program when all branchpoints are ignored now involves a while loop that keeps stepping until the program finishes:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 frame = {} 2 rest = lambda frame: top_level_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: (frame, None)) 3 while rest is not None: 4 frame, rest = rest(frame)
texts.text: 1 frame = {} 2 rest = lambda frame: top_level_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: (frame, None)) 3 while rest is not None: 4 frame, rest = rest(frame)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: And a simple DFS looks like this:
texts.text: And a simple DFS looks like this:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1 frame = {} 2 rest = lambda frame: top_level_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: (frame, None)) 3 stack = [(frame, rest)] 4 results = [] 5 while stack: 6 frame, rest = stack.pop() 7 for _ in range(branching_factor): 8 new_frame, new_rest = rest(frame.clone()) 9 if new_rest is None: 10 results.append(frame) 11 else: 12 stack.append((new_frame, new_rest))
texts.text: 1 frame = {} 2 rest = lambda frame: top_level_cps_function(frame, lambda frame: (frame, None)) 3 stack = [(frame, rest)] 4 results = [] 5 while stack: 6 frame, rest = stack.pop() 7 for _ in range(branching_factor): 8 new_frame, new_rest = rest(frame.clone()) 9 if new_rest is None: 10 results.append(frame) 11 else: 12 stack.append((new_frame, new_rest))

texts.text: We can wrap the state (frame, rest) into a Checkpoint object that provides a step method wrapping new_frame, new_rest = rest(frame.clone()) , and any search algorithm can now be implemented using the Checkpoint interface.

texts.orig: C.3 Other keywords
texts.text: C.3 Other keywords

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most other ENCOMPASS primitives provide auxiliary information, which we store in a dictionary info . We modify our transformation rules so that info always occurs alongside frame , so the Checkpoint object is now a wrapper around the 3-tuple (frame, info, rest) . The Checkpoint class implements the intended semantics of these additional ENCOMPASS keywords using the information stored inside info -details which we will omit.
texts.text: Most other ENCOMPASS primitives provide auxiliary information, which we store in a dictionary info . We modify our transformation rules so that info always occurs alongside frame , so the Checkpoint object is now a wrapper around the 3-tuple (frame, info, rest) . The Checkpoint class implements the intended semantics of these additional ENCOMPASS keywords using the information stored inside info -details which we will omit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that info is copied upon Checkpoint.step() similar to how frame gets cloned. In other words, stepping is now implemented as new_frame, new_info, new_rest = rest(frame .clone(), info.copy()) .
texts.text: Note that info is copied upon Checkpoint.step() similar to how frame gets cloned. In other words, stepping is now implemented as new_frame, new_info, new_rest = rest(frame .clone(), info.copy()) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For keywords that are used as standalone statements, preprocessing is done to convert these keywords into statements that modify info . The only exception is kill_branch() , which is transformed into a finish_callback() call. Here, we list the preprocessing transformations for all keywords that are used as standalone statements:
texts.text: For keywords that are used as standalone statements, preprocessing is done to convert these keywords into statements that modify info . texts.label: text
texts.orig: Without ENCOMPASS : Explicitly defining a state machine to support general search not only significantly obscures the underlying agent logic, but is also prone to bugs such as KeyError when accessing the dictionary cur_state that stores all the variables. A lot of newly added code is for bookkeeping to maintain a persistent state, which is implemented as a dictionary that stores the variables of the base agent.
texts.text: Without ENCOMPASS : Explicitly defining a state machine to support general search not only significantly obscures the underlying agent logic, but is also prone to bugs such as KeyError when accessing the dictionary cur_state that stores all the variables. A lot of newly added code is for bookkeeping to maintain a persistent state, which is implemented as a dictionary that stores the variables of the base agent.

texts.orig: 1 def reflexion_agent(task_info, internal_tests, max_iters): 2 # first attempt 3 code = solver_agent(task_info) 4 percent_correct, feedback = run_validation(code, internal_tests) 5 6 # if solved, exit early 7 if percent_correct == 1.0: 8 return code 9 10 for cur_iter in range(1, max_iters): 11 # self-reflect and apply to next attempt 12 reflection = self_reflection_agent(code, feedback) 13 code = solver_agent(task_info, code, feedback, reflection) 14 percent_correct, feedback = run_validation(code, internal_tests) 15 16 # if solved, exit early 17 if percent_correct == 1.0: 18 return code 19 20 return code 21 22 23 reflexion_agent(...)
